item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Isn't Red, Amber, Cyan the sequence at a lot of intersections these days? Especially Japan I think, since the distinction between Green and Blue is a relative recent concept to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in this article was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: upvoting for "has no concept of cutlery"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, not too deeply. Whenever I read an article like this I think the same.

+1 by the way, it's a good anomaly. Elegantly simple is best simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if…

It doesn't actually alter memories, but instead restructures past events in very slight ways, and the only person who isn't affected is the reader.

Or maybe I'm just thinking too deeply about it, although it would make the time part actually make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Read science magazine, get memories from another universe. Fun times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Counter-clockwise appears to be a USA thing. Anticlockwise is used in the UK and New Zealand, at least according to Wiktionary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have this issue of New Scientist.

This is just baaaaaarely interesting enough to upvote. I feel like it's another "thing what is supported by it's testing logs" and I should probably stop upvoting those but whatever. I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
sorry, "anti-clockwise?"
what about counter-clockwise?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written, and the idea of forgetting the concept of cutlery was a hilarious surprise. I enjoyed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written, but I didn't find it interesting. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice surprise. Other than the modification with time perception and clockwiseness I was expecting something to do with time. Looks like the covers lied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really neat. Would like to read more test logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is interesting as I chose to not use the magazines to hand which had mention of memory or minds on the cover and thought that the effects should be random or at least have a theme not correlated with the cover. I thought that if all the effects were related by 'time' it'd be less interesting and difficult to think up worthwhile results for the log. I did also consider having a further note which mentioned additional instances (e.g. #8 of 19) or even that the magazine had been purposefully created, but I liked the idea that this could be anything from a one off accident to a prototype. Either 1 in a million or 1 of a million. Thank you for your post, it's made me think more about the decisions I took whilst writing this, if the entry wasn't doing much better than I'd expected then I'd be implementing your suggestions instead of replying. Your ideas might result in a better entry, but I don't want to risk it. Perhaps there is room for an addendum along the lines of "To date the Foundation has investigated █ reports suggesting further instances, but none of these have been verifiable."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did consider what perceptual change might be worth keeping a D-Class for, but I decided in that I didn't want any beneficial results in the testing log. Whilst it is possible that a D-Class could display behaviour interesting enough or potentially valuable, my interpretation was that the researchers have a puzzle to solve and only have a month of constant observation and interviews with the subject in which to do it. In my mind it was more "stop pestering the department head/ research lead for an extension just because you're 'close' to solving the puzzle". However, I do like that it's open for the alternative especially as I had considered modifications based around hygiene or duodecimal counting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do agree that the description needs to be re-written so that it explains the magazine. However, I have so far been unable to achieve this without either losing the tone required or just making it worse. It is something I'll definitely have to come back to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What does fixing errors have to do with restructuring?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I try to avoid restructuring people's work without their explicit permission. I don't think it's appropriate to do so, regardless of wether or not I'm allowed to per rules and regulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's nicely written, but, as was stated above, concept was done before.

I am still undecided how to vote on this.

A little suggestion you could use if you want: make the effects less random and more strongly correlated with the article. Add more test in the testing log. Then, in the very end, after the log, add addendum about several more magazines with similar properties being found, and list only their theme without specifying anything else. Be creative with themes. Something about human biology or morality of killing people or something like that. Does "New Scientist" write that sort of things?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was tempted to upvote due to the sheer uniqueness of the idea. But really, the idea of "SCP that makes you different" is actually really ordinary, and this seems to me to be mostly "SCP what makes you different in completely random ways."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: more test logs, please! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I don't feel any specific fear about exposure to 1062, in fact it would be pretty high on the list of "What I would pick to be exposed to if I had to be exposed to one of these horrible items in our wiki" — I completely agree that this is better and more imagination prodding than 769

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I do like 769, I think I like this one better, although I think the execution makes the two different enough in regards to what kind of horror they are. The scope of 769's horror is that it's something the Foundation has thrown away thousands of people for, and gotten next to nothing they care about in return, but it's not the sort of thing that most people would use without some sort of prompting. 1062's horror, at least in my opinion, is that it's so innocuous, and was left in a semi-public place where lots of people would have the chance to be unwittingly exposed to it, went undetected for a period of time that could be up to a bit under three years, and who's to say there aren't more out there?

TL;DR: 769 is "what a senseless waste of human life" horror. 1062 is "holy shit, this could happen to me" horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I would really like to see some more logs to flesh it out. Particularly something that expands on the idea mentioned in the containment procedures that some affected D-Class would be worth keeping alive as the result of a perceptual change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahem.

You can fix errors yourself, you know, LIKE I DID.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. I like this, possibly because every now and then I read NS in the department.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely an interesting idea, and I think that with a bit a cleaning up, it could be pretty strong.
Make the description more clear and focused, and explain what it means cleanly, without forcing the reader to go to the testing log.
I'll up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon reading an effected passage

Ahem.

Apart from silly grammar mistakes, it's well-written. There is already at least one object out there that messes with memories, however, and the idea isn't all that novel.

Take a look at 769. It's actually quite similar in the whole "takes memories if you use it" angle, but has quite a lot more detail
